title: Stack and Monotonic Stack
description: java.util.Stack vs the recommended ArrayDeque-as-stack from Queue and Deque, plus the monotonic stack pattern for next-greater-element problems.
objectives: Use ArrayDeque as a stack, and know why it's preferred over java.util.Stack
Solve balanced parentheses (recap) and next greater element with a stack
Understand the monotonic stack pattern and why it's O(n)

sections.text: 1. Two Ways to Get a Stack in Java
In Queue and Deque, we previewed using ArrayDeque with push()/pop() for LIFO (stack) behavior. Java also has an older class, java.util.Stack -- but it's now considered legacy.

java.util.Stack extends Vector, an older, synchronized (thread-safety-overhead) class from early Java -- it works, but is not recommended for new code. ArrayDeque is faster and is Java's officially recommended stack implementation today.

We already solved this exact problem in Queue and Deque -- push every opening bracket, pop on a matching close, and check the stack is empty at the end. It's worth recognizing this is fundamentally a STACK problem: the most RECENT unmatched opener must be matched first, which is exactly LIFO order.
3. Next Greater Element: A New Stack Pattern

For each element, find the next element to its right that's GREATER than it. Brute force checks every pair -- O(n^2), like the Big-O Notation hasDuplicate example. A monotonic stack solves it in O(n).

A stack maintained so its elements are always in increasing (or decreasing) order -- new elements pop off anything on top that violates this order before being pushed, often computing something useful during each pop.

sections.title: Tracing nextGreaterElement({2,1,3,4,2})
steps: i=0 (val 2): stack empty -- push index 0. stack=[0]
i=1 (val 1): nums[1]=1 > nums[stack.peek()=0]=2? No -- push index 1. stack=[0,1]
i=2 (val 3): nums[2]=3 > nums[1]=1? Yes -- pop 1, result[1]=3. Now nums[2]=3 > nums[0]=2? Yes -- pop 0, result[0]=3. stack empty -- push index 2. stack=[2]
i=3 (val 4): nums[3]=4 > nums[2]=3? Yes -- pop 2, result[2]=4. stack empty -- push index 3. stack=[3]
i=4 (val 2): nums[4]=2 > nums[3]=4? No -- push index 4. stack=[3,4]
Loop ends. Indices 3 and 4 never found a greater element to their right -- their result stays -1

sections.title: Why this is O(n), not O(n^2)
points: Each index is pushed onto the stack EXACTLY ONCE
Each index is popped AT MOST ONCE
Total push+pop operations across the whole run: at most 2n -- O(n) overall, despite the nested while loop appearing to suggest otherwise

sections.text: The stack holds INDICES, not values -- this lets us write the answer directly into result[index] at the moment we pop, and still compare using nums[stack.peek()] for the actual value at that position.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using java.util.Stack in new code instead of the recommended ArrayDeque -- it still works, but isn't the modern preferred choice.
Storing VALUES on the monotonic stack instead of INDICES, losing the ability to write results back into the correct array position.
Assuming the nested while loop makes next-greater-element O(n^2) -- each element is pushed and popped at most once total, giving O(n) overall, not O(n) per outer iteration.
Forgetting to initialize the result array to a sentinel value (like -1) for elements that never find a next greater element.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: Why is ArrayDeque preferred over java.util.Stack for stack behavior in modern Java?
questions.answer: java.util.Stack extends the older, synchronized Vector class, carrying unnecessary thread-safety overhead for typical use. ArrayDeque is faster and is Java's officially recommended general-purpose stack implementation today.

questions.question: What is a monotonic stack?
questions.answer: A stack maintained so its elements stay in increasing or decreasing order -- when a new element would violate that order, elements are popped off (often computing a useful result during each pop) before the new one is pushed.

questions.question: Why is the next-greater-element algorithm O(n) despite having a nested while loop inside a for loop?
questions.answer: Each index is pushed onto the stack exactly once and popped at most once across the entire algorithm, so the total number of push/pop operations is bounded by O(n), not O(n) per outer iteration.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: ArrayDeque is Java's modern recommended stack implementation via push()/pop()/peek(), preferred over the legacy, Vector-based java.util.Stack. A monotonic stack maintains its elements in increasing or decreasing order, popping off violating elements (often computing a result during each pop) before pushing the new one. The classic example is next-greater-element: storing indices on the stack, popping whenever the current value is greater than the value at the stack's top index, and recording that as the popped index's answer. Despite a nested while loop, each index is pushed and popped at most once total, giving O(n) overall rather than O(n^2).

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Monotonic stack = a lineup of bouncers, shortest to tallest (or vice versa) -- when someone taller shows up, they KNOCK OUT everyone shorter below them first (that's their 'next greater'), before taking their own spot in line.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Why is ArrayDeque preferred over java.util.Stack?
What does 'monotonic' mean in the context of a monotonic stack?
Why does next-greater-element run in O(n) despite its nested while loop?
Why does the stack store indices rather than values in this pattern?

sections.title: Valid Parentheses
sections.problemStatement: Given a string s containing just '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid -- every open bracket must be closed by the same type, in the correct order.
hints: Push every opening bracket; on a closing bracket, it must match whatever is currently on TOP of the stack -- exactly the balanced-parentheses pattern previewed back in Queue and Deque.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Repeatedly find and remove any adjacent matching pair (like "()", "[]", "{}") until no more removals are possible; valid if the string becomes empty.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A stack: push opening brackets, and on a closing bracket, pop and check it matches.

sections.title: Remove All Adjacent Duplicates In String
sections.problemStatement: Given a string s, repeatedly remove adjacent duplicate letters until no more removals can be made, and return the final string.
hints: Push characters onto a stack; if the next character matches the stack's top, pop instead of pushing -- this handles cascading removals in a single pass.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Repeatedly scan for and remove one adjacent duplicate pair, restarting the scan, until no more removals are possible.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A single-pass stack (implemented with a StringBuilder acting as a stack): pop (delete last char) if it matches, otherwise push (append).

sections.title: Baseball Game
sections.problemStatement: Given a list of strings representing operations on a record of scores -- an integer (new score), "+" (sum of previous two), "D" (double the previous), or "C" (cancel/remove the previous) -- return the sum of all scores on the record after applying all operations.

examples.explanation: 5, 2, cancel 2 (back to just 5), double previous (5*2=10), sum of previous two (5+10=15). Record: [5,10,15]. Sum = 30.

hints: A stack naturally represents 'the record so far' -- "C" pops, "D"/"+" peek at recent entries before pushing a new one, and numbers just push directly.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Use an ArrayList instead of a stack, removing/inspecting from the end manually -- functionally identical to a stack, just via a different API.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: An ArrayDeque used as a stack -- push/peek/pop directly express each operation naturally.

sections.title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
sections.problemStatement: Evaluate an arithmetic expression given in Reverse Polish Notation (postfix). Valid operators are +, -, *, /. Each operand may be an integer or another expression's result.
hints: Push numbers; when an operator appears, pop the top TWO numbers, apply the operator, and push the result back -- classic stack-based expression evaluation.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Same fundamental approach is required for RPN -- there isn't a meaningfully simpler non-stack alternative; shown here using an ArrayList as the stack.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: An ArrayDeque as a stack -- push numbers, pop two and combine on each operator.

sections.title: Min Stack
sections.problemStatement: Design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and retrieving the minimum element, all in O(1) time.

examples.input: push(-2); push(0); push(-3); getMin(); pop(); top(); getMin()
examples.explanation: After popping -3, the stack is [-2, 0]; top is 0, and the new minimum is -2.

constraints: At most 3*10^4 calls to push, pop, top, and getMin
hints: Maintain a SECOND stack that tracks the minimum seen so far at each point -- pushed and popped in lockstep with the main stack, so getMin() is always just a peek.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: A single stack, with getMin() scanning the entire stack every time -- correct, but O(n) instead of O(1).
approaches.timeComplexity: O(1) for push/pop/top, O(n) for getMin

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two stacks in lockstep: the main stack, and a min-stack tracking the minimum value at each corresponding depth.

sections.title: Decode String
sections.problemStatement: Given an encoded string in the form k[encoded_string], decode it -- the encoded string inside brackets is repeated exactly k times, and this can be nested.

examples.explanation: 2[c] decodes to "cc", so a2[c] is "acc", and 3[a2[c]] repeats "acc" three times.

hints: Two stacks: one for pending repeat counts, one for pending partial strings -- when a ']' closes a bracket, pop both and repeat/append, exactly handling nested brackets naturally via the call stack of pushes/pops.

approaches.label: Brute Force (Recursive)
approaches.explanation: Recursively parse: read a number, expect '[', recursively decode until the matching ']', repeat that result, and continue.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Iterative with two explicit stacks (counts and partial strings) instead of recursion -- avoids relying on the call stack for nesting.

sections.title: Next Greater Element I
sections.problemStatement: Given two arrays nums1 and nums2 (nums1 is a subset of nums2), for each element of nums1, find its next greater element in nums2 (the first greater element to its right). If none exists, use -1.

examples.explanation: 4's next greater in nums2 doesn't exist (4 is nums2's last relevant max). 1's next greater is 3. 2's next greater doesn't exist.

constraints: nums2 consists of unique elements, nums1 is a subset
hints: Precompute next-greater for EVERY element of nums2 using the monotonic stack pattern from this lesson's Section 3, storing results in a HashMap, then look up each nums1 element.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each element of nums1, scan forward in nums2 from its position to find the first greater value.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A monotonic stack (exactly this lesson's Section 3 pattern) computes next-greater for all of nums2 in one pass, stored in a HashMap for O(1) lookup per nums1 element.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(m) for the stack and map

sections.title: Next Greater Element II
sections.problemStatement: Given a CIRCULAR integer array nums, return the next greater element for every element -- the array wraps around, so the search for a greater element can continue from the beginning.

examples.explanation: For the last 1, wrapping around finds 2 at index 0 as its next greater element.

hints: Simulate the circular wraparound by iterating TWICE through the array (index i % n), while only pushing/recording results during the conceptual first pass.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each index, scan forward up to n-1 additional steps (wrapping with modulo) looking for a greater value.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Monotonic stack over indices, iterating 2n times (i % n) to simulate the circular wraparound.

sections.title: Daily Temperatures
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of daily temperatures, return an array answer where answer[i] is the number of days you'd have to wait after day i to get a warmer temperature. If there is no future day, answer[i] = 0.
hints: This is next-greater-element, but returning the DISTANCE (index difference) to the next greater element instead of its value.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each day, scan forward until a warmer day is found.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A monotonic stack of indices -- when a warmer day is found, pop and compute the index difference for every day it resolves.

sections.title: Online Stock Span
sections.problemStatement: Design an algorithm that collects daily stock price quotes and returns the SPAN of the stock's price for the current day -- the number of consecutive days (including today) the price has been less than or equal to today's price.

examples.input: next(100); next(80); next(60); next(70); next(60); next(75); next(85)
examples.explanation: For 75: prices 70,60,75 back to (but not past) 60... spans back through 60,70 which are <=75, plus the earlier 80 breaks it. Span=4 covers 60,70,60,75.

hints: Maintain a monotonic stack of (price, span) pairs -- when a new price arrives, pop and ACCUMULATE the span of every previous price that's <= the new one, since they're all now part of this price's span too.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Store all prices in a list; for each new price, scan backward counting consecutive prices <= it.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A monotonic stack storing (price, span) pairs -- popping and accumulating spans whenever the new price is >= the stack's top.

sections.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of integers heights representing the histogram's bar heights where each bar has width 1, return the area of the largest rectangle that can be formed within the histogram.

examples.explanation: The largest rectangle has height 5 and width 2 (bars at indices 2,3 with heights 5,6 -- limited to height 5), giving area 10.

hints: A monotonic (increasing) stack of indices: when a shorter bar is found, it means every taller bar still on the stack has just found its RIGHT boundary -- pop and compute that bar's maximal rectangle area.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every pair of bars, compute the rectangle limited by the shorter of the two, tracking the maximum.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A monotonic increasing stack of indices -- popping a taller bar when a shorter one arrives lets us compute its maximal rectangle using the new shorter bar's position as the right boundary.

sections.title: Trapping Rain Water
sections.problemStatement: Given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map, compute how much water it can trap after raining. (Same problem as covered under Two Pointers -- reframed here with a monotonic-stack based solution.)
hints: A monotonic decreasing stack of indices -- when a taller bar arrives, it forms the RIGHT wall of a trapped-water region, with the stack's new top as the left wall and the just-popped index as the bottom.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Same brute force as the Two Pointers version -- for every index, scan both directions for maxLeft/maxRight.

approaches.label: Optimal (Monotonic Stack)
approaches.explanation: A monotonic decreasing stack of indices -- popping the bottom of a 'valley' whenever a taller bar arrives, computing trapped water bounded by the new top (left wall) and current index (right wall).
